 I was just reading an [article](https://www.reuters.com/article/uk-soccer-brazil-stadiums-idUKKBN12H21F) about that time when Flamengo's stadium was being renovated so instead of playing in another Rio stadium like their local fans would have wanted, they decided to tour the country in order to sell presumably more expensive tickets on the basis that it was a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity to see them playing "home games" in Natal for example, which is 2,500km away from Rio
 
 >“The anger is subsiding because we know that we will be back next week but it has been a hard year,” said Fernando Lamego, a Flamengo supporter who has not seen his team all year. “In Rio de Janeiro, the fans are not happy.”
 
 >The Maracana has been out of commission for much of the past 20 years, being refurbished for first the World Club Cup in 2000, then the 2007 Pan American games and finally this year’s Rio Olympics.
 
 >They have played 15 “home” league games, none of them at the Maracana or the Olympic stadium, which is owned by rivals Botafogo.
 
 >Lamego, who has a season ticket and hopes to go to Flamengo versus Corinthians on Sunday, has, like most fans, been forced to watch games on television instead.

text:
> The European players weren't all equally as good but they were of a similar level and the best ones were just as good, or slightly better or slightly not as good.
 
 I think you are mistaking "European players" with what at the time were "domestic" players? Maybe I explained it poorly. If you take "European players" as a whole, sure, there was good Europeans and good South Americans and good Africans. But that was not the case. You have to compare Spanish players vs Euro+SA+Africans, English vs Euro+SA+Africans, etc.
 
 Simplifying (cause there were small exceptions) if you played for Real Madrid, you had to be Spanish to be considered domestic. If you were Italian, German, Brazilian or Chinese you counted as a foreigner.
 
 Real Madrid was limited to the Spanish talent pool. If they had a good generation maybe they could buy some good players, but if they didn't then you had to do with what you had. And this happened on every team. 
 
 So even if Real Madrid _all_ the best Spanish players, it's likely that they still wouldn't have the quality of the top players from over the world. Think the best players in the world today, where are they from? Same country? No.
 
 That means that the foreigners you bought, were going to be the 3 best players possible to maximize the spots you had. That meant that you would get players that had a better level than the ones from your team if you can. And unless the Spanish generation had the best players in the world, it did mean that the foreigners were better.
 
 And that's Real Madrid. Now imagine second tier teams. Real Madrid and Barca bought _all_ the best Spanish players. The quality from the second tiers is gonna be worse. But since only 6 foreigners could be bought by RM and Barca, they had the chance to get really good quality players that were significantly better than their squad. The local talent pool has one size, meanwhile the worldwide one is bigger, so it's 100% going to be better, unless you have a golden generation.
 
 So, overall, unless it was a particularly good domestic generation, most teams had foreigners (which include Europeans as well as non Europeans) that were likely to be the best players on their teams, because it simply made sense to do things that way.
 
 > It's not the same like Benzema going to the Saudi league where the majority aren't just not as good as him they're division 2 or division 3 players
 
 I didn't say the gap isn't bigger in Saudi. I don't follow the league so I don't know, but yeah, I assume Benzema is better than them significantly. 
 
 But I replied to someone saying the players were just as a good and that wasn't true. There was a gap in talent from foreigners to local, that was very very obvious at the time.
label: hot_take
reasoning: Though the person seems to try to make a cohesive argument, there is a lack of concrete evidence. So, this will be a Hot Take.
